draft.introduction: Analects 2:4 is Confucius's famous intellectual autobiography, tracing his spiritual development from age fifteen to seventy. It became the template for understanding the Confucian path of self-cultivation as a lifelong process.

translation.text: The Master said: "At fifteen I set my heart on learning. At thirty I took my stand. At forty I had no doubts. At fifty I knew the will of Heaven. At sixty my ear was attuned. At seventy I could follow my heart's desire without overstepping the boundaries of right."
translation.original_text: 子曰：吾十有五而志于学，三十而立，四十而不惑，五十而知天命

translator_notes.note: The climactic stage—"follow my heart's desire without overstepping the boundaries"—describes a state where moral action has become completely spontaneous. This is the Confucian equivalent of the Taoist wu wei: not the absence of rules but such thorough internalization of them that compliance requires no effort. It parallels Aristotle's account of the virtuous person for whom right action is pleasant.
